name: equator_june_solstice
source: Sun's declination at the June solstice is the obliquity of the ecliptic, ~23.44deg (IAU/USNO). Altitude at local solar noon follows the standard identity alt = 90 - |lat - decl|.

name: mid_latitude_nh_june_solstice
source: Same obliquity constant and noon-altitude identity as equator_june_solstice, evaluated at a mid-northern latitude.

name: mid_latitude_sh_december_solstice
source: December solstice declination is -23.44deg; same noon-altitude identity evaluated at a mid-southern latitude during its summer solstice.

name: arctic_summer_polar_day
source: 78N is well above the Arctic Circle (~66.56N); the midnight sun at the June solstice is a standard fact (e.g. Svalbard almanac references).

name: arctic_winter_polar_night
source: Same latitude six months later, at the December solstice: polar night.

name: near_polar_89deg_june_solstice
source: At 89N the sun's altitude stays close to the declination all day (colatitude only 1deg); still polar day at the June solstice, with noon altitude near 90-|89-23.44|=24.44deg.

name: equator_equinox_rise_set
source: At the equator, day length is almost constant year-round; with the standard -0.833deg rise/set threshold the half-day is consistently ~6h03m, so sunrise/sunset sit close to 05:57/18:03 apparent solar time (e.g. USNO equator almanac tables).
